key_points:
## Asset Liability Management (ALM) — Directions, 2025 — Detailed Compliance Guide
*(RBI/DOR/2025-26/330, dated 2025-11-28)*

Framework for measuring, monitoring and managing an AIFI's **liquidity risk, interest rate risk and foreign-exchange risk** through disciplined balance-sheet management.

### Chapter I — Preliminary
- **Effective from date of issue.** **Applicability:** the five AIFIs (EXIM, NABARD, SIDBI, NHB, **NaBFID**).
- **Definitions:** **Interest Rate Risk** (adverse impact of rate changes on financial condition), **Gap** (Rate-Sensitive Assets − Rate-Sensitive Liabilities per time bucket), **Liquidity Risk** (inability to meet obligations as due), **Stress Test**.

### Chapter II — Role of the Board
- The **Board** owns risk management, sets the risk policy and limits for liquidity, interest-rate, exchange-rate and equity-price risks, and constitutes a Management Committee / **ALCO**.
- The **Board/ALCO** approve: Trading Book limits (volume, composition, max maturity/duration, holding, cut-loss); optionally an integrated **VaR** limit for the whole balance sheet; internal prudential limits for **cumulative negative liquidity gaps** and **interest-rate gaps** (as simple gap limits or as impact on NII/NIM/Earnings-at-Risk); and the time-bucket classification of assets/liabilities.

### Chapter III — ALM Governance
- ALM rests on **three pillars**: (1) **ALM Information System** (MIS + timely, accurate data), (2) **ALM Organisation** (structure + top-management involvement), (3) **ALM Process** (risk identification, measurement, management, policies/tolerances).
- **ALCO** is the decision-making unit for integrated balance-sheet management — headed by CEO/CMD/DMD/ED, with the heads of Investment, Credit, Resources, Treasury and Risk as members. It monitors risk limits, articulates the interest-rate view, and sets business/funding strategy. An **ALM Support Group** does the analysis, monitoring and simulations.

### Chapter IV — Liquidity Risk Management
- Assess **market** and **funding** liquidity risk; the Board sets **liquidity risk tolerance**; price liquidity into internal pricing and new-product approval.
- Use **early-warning indicators**, run regular **stress tests / scenario analysis**, and maintain a **contingency funding plan**.
- **Statement of Liquidity** (maturity-ladder approach, Annex I) across **10 time buckets** (1–14 days; 15–28 days; 29 days–3 months; 3–6m; 6m–1y; 1–3y; 3–5y; 5–7y; 7–10y; over 10y).
- **Negative gap limits:** the negative gap must not exceed **10%** of outflows in the 1–14 day bucket and **15%** in the 15–28 day bucket; cumulative mismatches monitored against Board/ALCO limits.
- **Trading Book** securities: defined composition, restricted max maturity/duration, **holding period ≤ 90 days**, cut-loss limits, and daily/weekly mark-to-market with gains/losses to P&L.

### Chapter V — Management of Currency Risk
- Prepare **currency-wise** Statements of Liquidity and Interest Rate Sensitivity for each currency of exposure (Annex I-A / II-A).

### Chapter VI — Interest Rate Risk Management (IRR)
- Two lenses: **earnings perspective** (impact on NII/NIM) and **economic-value perspective** (impact on Market Value of Equity / net worth).
- Use **Traditional Gap Analysis (TGA)** as the baseline; advanced techniques (Duration Gap, Simulation, VaR) with Board/ALCO approval.
- Prepare the **Interest Rate Sensitivity statement** (Annex II) across defined time buckets; set Board/ALCO **prudential gap limits** (related to Total/Earning Assets or Equity, or to EaR/NIM).

### Chapter VII — General
- **Behavioural pattern & embedded options:** better-equipped AIFIs may slot cash flows by behavioural maturity (with ALCO/Board approval and a note to RBI DoS); otherwise slot embedded-option amounts to the earliest exercise date.
- **Internal Transfer Pricing (ITP):** a scientifically designed mechanism to value funds provided/used, centralise interest-rate risk management, and price assets/liabilities consistently.

### Chapter VIII — Monitoring and Reporting
- **Statement of Liquidity — fortnightly.** **Interest Rate Sensitivity statement — monthly.** Top Management/Board/ALCO review regularly. **Both statements submitted to RBI (DoS) quarterly.**

### Chapter IX — Repeal and Other Provisions
- Repeals prior AIFI ALM instructions (circular dated 2025-11-28); standard saving clause; RBI's interpretation is final.

**Annexes I–IV** provide the prescribed formats: Statement of Liquidity (₹ and foreign currency), Interest Rate Sensitivity statement, the Maturity Profile (how each asset/liability head is slotted into time buckets), and the Interest Rate Sensitivity profile (which items are rate-sensitive and when they re-price).

_Verify with the compliance team before acting._
implications:
**Why it matters for NaBFID:** ALM is central to NaBFID's model — it **borrows via bonds (shorter/medium tenor) and lends 15–20 years to infrastructure**, so it structurally runs long-dated assets against shorter liabilities. This direction forces that mismatch to be measured (liquidity and interest-rate gaps), bounded (the 10%/15% short-bucket negative-gap limits, Board/ALCO gap limits) and governed (ALCO, stress tests, contingency funding plan), with fortnightly/monthly statements and quarterly filing to RBI.

*Illustrative example:* If NaBFID funds a 18-year power-project loan with 5-year bonds, the loan sits in the 'over 10 years' inflow bucket while the bond maturity sits in the '3–5 years' outflow bucket — creating a structural gap ALCO must monitor. The short-term negative liquidity gap in the 1–14 day bucket must stay within 10% of outflows, and the interest-rate re-pricing mismatch feeds the NII/EaR limits the Board has set (RBI/DOR/2025-26/330, dated 2025-11-28).

_Verify with the compliance team before acting._
